title-group.article-title: Several models combined with ultrasound techniques to predict breast muscle weight in broilers

contrib-group.aff: ⁎Institute of Animal Sciences, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences, Beijing, China
†Mile Xinguang Agricultural and Animal Industrials Corporation, Mile, China

fn.p: These authors contributed equally to this work.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

abstract.p: The weight of breast muscle (WBM) is a highly monitored indicator in broiler breeding that can be obtained after slaughtering. Currently, due to the lack of accurate in vivo phenotypes for both genomic and phenotypic selection, genetic gains in WBM fall short of initial expectations. In this study, 1,006 market-age (42 d) broilers from 3 generations over 2 yr were randomly selected, and the breast width (BW), fossil bone length (FBL), breast muscle thickness (BMT), and live weight (LW) were measured exactly in vivo. Eight models, including multiple linear regression (MLR), ridge regression (RR), least absolute shrinkage and selection operator (LASSO), and elastic net (EN), were fitted to explore the best regression relationships between breast muscle weight and these indicators. Support vector machine (SVM) methods with both linear kernels and radial kernels were used to fit the models, while 2 decision tree-based machine learning algorithms, random forest (RF), and extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost), were used to establish the prediction model. The predictive effects of different combinations of independent variables were compared, leading to the conclusion that the EN model achieves the best predictive power when all 4 live features are used as inputs and is slightly better than the other models (R2 = 0.7696). This method could be applied in practical production and breeding work, leading to substantial cost savings and enhancements in the breeding process.

sec.title: INTRODUCTION
sec.p: Chicken is the most consumed meat worldwide, and the most consumed meat in China after pork. In recent years, with the rise of new trends such as healthy and light food diets, chicken breast has been increasingly favored by young people because of its low-fat calories, high protein content, and moderate price (Kong et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2022). Breast weight is a carcass trait whose true data can only be obtained after slaughter, which means that accurate traits cannot be retained for breeding purposes. The current method of selection for pectoral weight is to obtain the weight of breast muscle by slaughtering broilers from the reference herd and then extrapolating the breeding value of individuals in the candidate herd from the pedigree or single nucleotide polymorphism (SNP) data (Tan et al., 2023). The above method is a time-consuming and expensive process that greatly hinders breeding and the improvement of carcass traits (Pewan et al., 2021). Therefore, an efficient and reliable phenotypic evaluation method that allows the retention of individuals with outstanding performance for breeding purposes is needed.

With the aim of predicting the weight of breast muscle, measuring breast thickness is inevitable. However, it is an in vivo trait that is not measurable by manual measurement techniques. Ultrasonic diagnostic technology is a nondestructive, immediate, convenient, and fast detection technique and has been widely used in the production measurement of various agricultural animals and carcass evaluation. In large livestock such as swine, cattle and rams, the time and financial costs of slaughter are even higher. However, this technology is used to help examine traits such as backfat thickness and eye muscle for the purpose of carcass evaluation and breeding, dramatically increasing the genetic gain (Choy et al., 2015; Roongsitthichai et al., 2021; Siachos et al., 2021). The application of ultrasound technology for the indirect measurement of organs such as poultry breast muscles, abdominal fat, and ovaries have also been reported (Oviedo-Rondon et al., 2007; Lin et al., 2018; Adegbenjo et al., 2020). Apart from pectoral muscle thickness, there are numerous traits that are considered to be associated with breast muscle weight. Fossil bone length (FBL) and breast width (BW) are 2 of the most direct external manifestations of the size of the breast muscle and have shown a strong correlation with breast muscle weight in previous studies. Live weight (LW), one of the best manually measurable live traits, has been found to strongly correlate with the weight of the carcass, legs, wings, and breast muscle in poultry (Laudadio et al., 2009). In one study, other live traits, such as shank length, body slope length, shank girth, and neck length, all showed correlations with breast weight and were used to establish a prediction model (Chen et al., 2023). Nevertheless, selecting too many independent variables would result in an increasing expenditure of time and personnel during measurement, defeating the original purpose.

Using the features measured from living broilers, an algorithm must be chosen to transform the input data to the weight of these individuals. There are currently various simple or complex algorithms with different unique advantages to choose from. A commonly used and proven model is known as multiple linear regression (MLR), which involves predicting a dependent variable with multiple characteristics and is generally applied to establish models due to its features, such as being simple and easily interpreted. MLR was used to establish a prediction model for lean meat percentage (LMP) in swine carcasses and was evaluated as a more robust and accurate method of prediction from the results (Lohumi et al., 2018). Alves et al. imported 16 live features into the MLR algorithm to build a predictive model for lamb carcass traits, achieving excellent fit results (Alves et al., 2019). Adding a penalty term after the loss term in the ordinary least squares method, penalty regression adjusts the coefficients in models, addressing the problems of overfitting and poor generalization of MLR (Wang et al., 2019). Penalty regression includes ridge regression (RR), least absolute shrinkage and selection operator (LASSO), and elastic net (EN), which are all widely applied to establish prediction models, such as predicting the intramuscular fat of steer in combination with machine scanning images (Jung et al., 2015), or the carcass structures of Japanese black steers with in vivo features (Maeno et al., 2014). A higher-dimensional feature space is mapped from the original input data by kernel functions in the support vector machine (SVM), easily solving the small-P-big-N problem. SVM is one of the most popular machine learning algorithms and has shown satisfactory results in numerous studies (Fan et al., 2022). In a recent study on the prediction of breast muscle weight, SVM achieved better results than an artificial neural network (ANN) (Chen et al., 2023). Random forest (RF), where the output of the model is represented by the voting result of many decision trees, is another typical machine learning approach. An RF prediction model was established to estimate the carcass weight of cattle with several relevant features, and it performed better than a multilayer neural network (MNN) (Aiken et al., 2020). Extreme gradient boosting (XGBoost) is an advanced decision tree algorithm used to analyze computed tomography imaging in human medicine, particularly for various cancers, and it has achieved better results than K-nearest neighbor (KNN) and generalized linear model (GLM) (Nazari et al., 2021; Nageswaran et al., 2022). In conclusion, the performance exhibited among the models varies when faced with different kinds of problems, so it is necessary to proceed with the tests to determine the optimal solution in this study.

To conclude, the aim of this study is to find a stable and reliable forecasting approach for breast muscle in vivo, including the best suitable method of model building and the optimal combination of independent variables, which can save a tremendous amount of time, cash, and labor costs and provide feasible methodological guidance for actual production.

sec.title: Collection of Animals and Traits
sec.p: In this study, 42-day-old individuals were randomly selected from the 9th, 10th, and 11th generations of fast-growing broilers reared by Mile Xinguang Agricultural and Animal Industrials Co., Ltd. (Mile, China). All animal procedures were approved by the Animal Ethics Committee of the Institute of Animal Sciences, Chinese Academy of Agricultural Sciences (IAS-CAAS, Beijing, China). All broilers were raised in staircase cages with separate fences and free access to water and food under the same recommended environmental and nutritional conditions (Feeding standards of broilers, China, NY 33-2004).

Broilers were weighed after feeding was stopped for 12 h to obtain the LW, and the measurement for phenotypic traits was performed according to the Poultry Performance terminology and measurements for poultry (China, NYT823-2020). The distance between the shoulder joints on each side was measured as the BW, while the distance from the front end of the keel to the end of the keel was measured as the FBL. Breast muscle thickness (BMT) was measured with a Mylab Touch VET B-ultrasound diagnostic instrument (Shenzhen Baisheng Medical Technology Co., Ltd., Shenzhen, China) with a 5.0 cm linear array probe, and performed according to the following procedure: The probe was positioned approximately 2 cm outward from the midpoint of the keel. Its front end was facing toward the head of the chicken, placed in a vertical orientation to the surface of the breast. The probe was then slowly adjusted until a distinct bright white line appeared in the captured ultrasound image. This image was saved and the distance between the skin (located at the top of the image) and the sternum (represented by the white line) was measured and recorded as the thickness of chest (Figure S1).

After the collection of traits was completed, the chickens were electroshocked, bled for 5 min, scalded with hot water at 60°C and plucked. After death, breast muscle muscles on both sides were stripped and weighed as the weight of breast muscle (WBM).

sec.title: Establishment of Models
sec.p: After all data were collected, descriptive statistics were performed on the in vivo data, and correlation analysis was performed on the traits to calculate the Pearson correlation coefficient between each trait:(1)r=N∑xiyi−∑xi∑yiN∑xi2−(∑xi)2N∑yi2−(∑yi)2
We then randomly divided the training and test sets at a 4:1 ratio. The training set was used to build the model before testing the predictive power of the model with the test set.

In a MLR model, the model is fitted following the principle of least squares to minimize the sum of squares of the errors between the prediction results and the actual data. In MLR, a strong linear relationship between the independent variables can lead to multiple cointegration, resulting in an unreliable model. This can be tested by calculating the variance inflation factor (VIF). Factors with VIF > 10 will be identified as strong collinearity factors and should be deleted.(2)VIFi=11−Ri2where Ri2 refers to the coefficient of determination after MLR of feature i using other independent variables.

The overfitting effect, where there is a strong fit to the training set data and an inadequate prediction of the test set, can occur in a MLR model, where all the independent variables are selected. To increase the generalization ability of the model, a penalty term can be added to the loss function to shrink the coefficients, reduce the complexity of the model, and prevent overfitting. Among these 3 penalty regressions, RR uses l2 regularization to reduce the Euler distance (∑i=1n(xi1+xi2)2) between the data and increases the penalty term λ||β||22 on the coefficients:(3)minL(β)=(y−Xβ)T(y−Xβ)+λ||β||22

RR can reduce the coefficients to close to zero but not to zero, thus enhancing the fit of the model. However, RR cannot reduce the coefficients of the features to zero, cannot make an effective selection of the independent variables, and requires a large amount of computation when the number of features is large.
The LASSO uses l1 regularization to calculate the Manhattan distance (∑i=1n|xi1+xi2|) between sample points and adds a penalty term λ||β||22:(4)minL(β)=(y−Xβ)T(y−Xβ)+λ||β||1

LASSO regression causes the coefficients of independent variables with small coefficients to become zero, provides a stronger choice of features, facilitates model interpretation, and enhances the generalization ability of the model to high-dimensional data, especially the sparse model, which has better predictive power.

EN combines the characteristics of both penalty regression algorithms by introducing a parameter α and adding 2 regularized penalty terms, l1 and l2, according to certain weights, which degenerate to LASSO if α = 1 and to RR if α = 0:(5)minL(β)=(y−Xβ)T(y−Xβ)+αλ||β||1+(1−α)λ||β||22

EN models combine the advantages of both penalty regression algorithms, retaining the excellent fit of RR while being selective about features. All these penalty regression models are built by the corresponding function from the glmnet package in R.

SVM uses the kernel function technique to map low-dimensional data features to a high-dimensional data space through the kernel function technique, and a hyperplane is built in this high-dimensional space with the closest distance to all sample points. The hyperplane is used to solve classification and regression problems. Commonly used kernel functions include linear kernels, polynomial kernels, radial kernels, sigmoid kernels, etc. In this study, we used the e1071 package and rely on both linear and Gaussian kernels for model building.(6)linearkernel:k(xi,xj)=xiTxj(7)radialkernel:k(xi,xj)=e(−|xi−xj|22σ2),σ>0

As a variant of decision trees, a RF is based on the idea of bagging for integrated learning and consists of many weak learners. A RF regression model is built by generating many regression trees, and the prediction of the model is the average of the predicted values of all trees. The RF regression model was built using the randomForest package in R.(8)yi=yi1+yi2+yi3+···+yinn

XGBoost is another variant of decision trees. It improves on the gradient boosting algorithm by generating a decision tree. Each new tree builds on the existing tree to move closer to the target, reducing the prediction error. The output of the model is the sum of all decision tree results. XGBoost improves the speed of operation of nonlinear data and reduces the degree of overfitting. It has been applied in medical imaging processing and has a better analysis ability. The XGBoost regression model was built using the XGBoost package in R.yi1=0+f1(xi)yi2=yi1+f2(xi)=f1(xi)+f2(xi)(9)yit=yit−1+ft(xi)=∑k=1tfk(xi)
In this study, the hyperparameters of all the models were determined by k-fold cross-validation (k = 10) with the caret package in R.

sec.title: Evaluation of Prediction Ability
sec.p: After building the model, we evaluated the predictive power of the model using 4 indicators: coefficient of determination (R2), mean square error (MSE), root square mean error (RMSE), mean absolute error (MAE), and mean absolute percentage error (MAPE).(10)R2:R2=1−SSESST=1−∑i=1n(yi−y^i)2∑i=1n(yi−y¯)2(11)RMSE:RMSE=1n∑i=1n(yi−y^i)2(12)MAE:MAE=1n∑i=1n|yi−y^i|(13)MAPE:MAPE=100n∑i=1n|yi−y^i|yi%

sec.title: Statistical Analysis
sec.p:
Descriptive statistics of broiler phenotype information are shown in Table 1, and the values outside the range of 3 times the standard deviation of the mean were excluded as outliers. Thus, 1,006 individuals remained. Among the randomly selected broilers, the mean breast weight was 535.31 g, with a coefficient of variation of 17.13%. The coefficient of variation (CV) values for BMT, keel length, and BW were lower, with values of 8.29, 4.28, and 5.35%, respectively; meanwhile, the coefficient of variation for the LW characteristic was comparable to that of breast weight, with a value of 16.81%.Table 1
Means, standard deviation (SD), coefficient of variation (CV), and ranges (Min = minimum; Max = maximum) for independent variable and dependent variable.Table 1
VarsMeanSDCV (%)MinMax
Breast muscle thickness (cm)3.990.328.123.005.00
Breast width (cm)10.480.524.958.9012.10
Fossil bone length (cm)15.510.644.1313.6017.20
Live weight (g)2485.16394.5615.881610.003760.00
Weight of breast muscle (g)531.9587.2916.41312.66809.26

sec.title: Correlation Analysis
sec.p:
The results of correlation analysis on each broiler trait (Figure 1) showed that the breast muscle weight was strongly correlated with BMT, chest width, FBL, and LW traits. The strongest correlation was 0.819, which supported the rationality of using these independent variables to predict the breast muscle weight and was consistent with existing reports (Swatland, 1989; Chen et al., 2023). Among the independent variables, the strong correlation between the FBL and LW may be detrimental to the MLR analysis.Figure 1
Correlation analysis between breast muscle thickness (BMT), breast width (BW), fossil bone length (FBL), live weight (LW), and weight of breast muscle (WBM).Figure 1

sec.title: Tuning of Hyperparameters
sec.p: In the MLR model, the VIF was calculated to determine whether the occurrence of multicollinearity in the model can be ignored (BMT = 1.190789, BW = 1.195548, FBL = 1.561083, and LW = 1.622628, which are all below the normal level). The penalty regression model was built from the corresponding function in the glmnet package and used 10-fold cross-validation to select the optimal parameters. In the RR models, the MSE is minimized when lambda = 7.66418, while LASSO regression models achieve the best predictive effect when lambda = 0.3804539. When building the elastic network regression model, we used the grid search method to determine that the best prediction results are obtained when lambda = 0.435194 and alpha = 0.2, which means that the weight of the l1:l2 regularization method is 2:8.

The support vector regression models were established by selecting the commonly used radial kernel and linear kernel as kernel functions. To select the most suitable parameters for building the models, a lattice-based search method under a 5-fold cross-validation framework was used. The optimal parameters found were sigma = 0.05 and C = 0.21 for the Gaussian kernel SVR model, cost = 0.07 for the linear kernel model, and the default values for the other parameters.

The RF regression model was built using the randomForest package in R. The error was minimized at 250 trees. In the XGBoost regression model, the optimal parameters were selected by a grid search method: eta = 0.1, max_depth = 2, subsample = 0.5, colsample_bytree = 1, gamma = 0.53, min_child_weight = 1, and the other parameters were set by default.

sec.title: Comparison of Models
sec.p:
In this study, regression models of breast weight from in vivo data were established with 4 live features using 8 algorithms. The distribution of the predicted results of each model and the actual values of breast weight are shown in Figure 2. It is observed that all 8 models have a satisfactory prediction ability of the breast muscle weight, while the differences between them are not significant. The box plots of R2 are shown in Figure 3, where it can be noted that the MLR, LASSO, RR, and EN models have better predictive effects compared with those of the other models, while their performance is similar to each other. SVM (with both linear and radial kernels), RF, and XGBoost have obviously worse prediction ability.Figure 2
Scatter plot of model predictions vs. true values, with points on the y = x line indicating that the predicted value is equal to the true value, and points above the line indicating that the predicted value is higher than the true value, otherwise it is lower than the true value.Figure 2Figure 3
The box plots of R2 for all models with 4 live traits as inputs, plots represent the result outputted from each prediction with test set.Figure 3

As seen in Table 2, the best choice of all 8 prediction models for breast muscle weight is EN, which obtained the highest R2 and least error (R2 = 0.7696, RMSE = 43.93, MAE = 33.10, and MAPE = 6.242%). The remaining models were ranked according to the coefficient of determination from largest to smallest: MLR (0.7662), LASSO (0.7662), RR (0.7630), SVM_linear (0.7531), XGBoost (0.7461), and SVM_radial (0.7370). RF was the worst prediction algorithm in this study, achieving the lowest R2 (0.7217) as well as the largest error indicators (RMSE = 45.96, MAE = 33.99, and MAPE = 6.434%) on this problem.Table 2
The root mean square error (RMSE), mean absolute error (MAE), and mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) of all 8 prediction models with all features.Table 2
MethodsRMSEMAEMAPE
MLR44.2733.306.278%
LASSO44.2833.306.278%
RR44.6233.496.314%
EN43.9333.106.242%
SVM_linear43.2632.446.164%
SVM_radial44.6733.266.304%
RF45.9633.996.434%
XGBoost43.9032.696.206%

sec.title: Selection of Variables
sec.p:
A stepwise back selection of variables was carried out in the process of establishing models to ensure that the correct combination of features was used in predictions. In terms of Shapley additive explanations (SHAP), which were exported from the XGBoost model presented in Figure 4 and reflect the relative contribution for the change in dependent variable, the length of the fossil bone is the least conducive feature in assisting predictions. However, as seen in Figure 5, from the perspective of the increase in node purity (IncNodePurity) output by the RF model, which is used to rank the importance of variables, the width of the breast has the least effect on the weight of the pectoral muscle and could be excluded from the model for examination.Figure 4
The Shapley additive explanations (SHAP) scores of every feature from XGBoost model.Figure 4Figure 5
The increase in node purity (IncNodePurity) of every variables in RF model.Figure 5

To investigate whether further simplification of the model could potentially improve the predictive power of breast muscle weight, we input all 8 models using the datasets excluding the FBL and the BW and observed the change in predictive power of the excluded variables relative to the full set of independent variables, the results of which are displayed in Table 3.Table 3
The relative change of R2 from models with independent variables with FBL excluded or BW excluded (treat the R2 from models with all variables as 100%).Table 3
MethodsFBL excludedBW excluded
MLR−1.97%−4.46%
LASSO−2.00%−4.07%
RR−1.83%−3.97%
EN−2.16%−4.18%
SVM_linear−0.37%−2.47%
SVM_radial−0.24%−1.09%
RF0.30%−2.16%
XGBoost−0.35%−3.30%

From the results, we can see that the removal of the length of the fossil bone led to a more or less significant reduction in R2 in all the remaining models, except the RF, in which a small improvement was obtained (0.30%). The reduction in the predictive power of the model with the removal of the BW was even more pronounced, with all algorithms showing a reduction in R2 ranging from 1.09 to 4.46%. In terms of results, the removal of the BW feature led to the emergence of worse predictive power than the removal of the length of the fossil bone, but almost all models with both combinations of reduced features showed entirely weaker predictive power than the models that retained the combination of the 4 independent variables. In this case, it can be concluded that retaining all 4 in vivo features measured as input data to the models is the optimal strategy.

sec.title: DISCUSSION
sec.p: The descriptive statistics and correlation analysis for the traits showed that these variables are all normally distributed, demonstrating the authenticity and credibility of the data, and satisfying the conditions of MLR. After correlation analysis for each phenotypic trait, we found that breast muscle weight had the strongest correlation with LW data (r = 0.819) and a strong correlation with the length, width, and thickness of breast muscle (r = 0.553, 0.410, and 0.516, respectively), suggesting a strong linear relationship between the independent and dependent variables in this problem, which is consistent with previous reports. This linear relationship is beneficial for methods such as MLR in terms of model fitting and prediction. However, for decision tree-based models, it may limit them from achieving their best performance (Liu et al., 2022; Dai et al., 2023). In this dataset, the independent variables showed strong correlations with each other; in particular, the correlation coefficient between keel length and LW reached 0.590, which was even higher than the correlation between breast muscle weight and most of the independent variables. The strong correlation between the independent variables may have a positive effect on the predictive effect of some of the algorithms, but for the MLR, it may lead to the occurrence of multicollinearity, resulting in an unacceptable model (Acosta-Jimenez et al., 2022).

A comparison of the best combination of independent variables was conducted between all variables and the combination with the worst independent variable, excluding the RF or XGBoost algorithm. The results showed that removing any variables leads to a decrease in predictive ability. This result suggests that all 4 features we chose in the study are essential for predicting breast weight, which is consistent with the above correlation analysis and in line with previous studies (Kleczek et al., 2006; Jin-lan et al., 2021). Sixteen in vivo measured features were used in Chen's study to predict the weight of breast muscle, and they achieved an even better effect than that in this study (Chen et al., 2023). However, excessive measurements can significantly increase the labor and time costs of workers and lead to a less feasible solution for practical production. Considering both operability and the accuracy of prediction, the 4 in vivo traits selected for this experiment would be a more appropriate strategy.

With all 4 independent variables, the MLR model and penalty regression models (RR, LASSO, and EN) showed better prediction effects than those of the machine learning models. The MLR model even outperformed most of the machine learning and penalty regression algorithms except EN in terms of predictive ability. This is perhaps due to the lower dimensionality of the independent variables and the low complexity of the problem (Kleczek et al., 2006). The EN regression algorithm performed the best among these methods. This is perhaps because the EN algorithm, which uses the l1 and l2 constraints, is considered by researchers to be capable of retaining and exploiting the correlation between features (Liu et al., 2013), while there is a strong correlation between the independent variable trait correlation in these data, as evidenced by the result of the above correlation analysis.

The poor performance of machine learning models in this problem seems to be related to the fact that fewer variables were selected, resulting in a problem that is too simple and linear (Stumpf et al., 2020). The unsatisfactory performance of SVM in this study is likely attributed to the fact that SVM models require a certain number of features mapped into higher-dimensional vectors to create a reasonable hyperplane, which could not be achieved in this scenario (Singla et al., 2011; Yang et al., 2018). The low feature variety may also lead to the underperformance of the decision tree variants, that is, RF and XGBoost, by reducing aspects of the model generalization ability (Liang et al., 2019). In other words, it was also reported that the vulnerability to overfitting that exists with decision trees can be exposed more clearly in the simple dataset used in this study (Jiang et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022).

Furthermore, the MLR model outperformed the SVM (linear kernel) in this study in terms of R2, but the errors that occurred in the regression of the SVM model with a linear kernel were smaller than those in the MLR, including the RMSE, MAE, and MAPE. This indicates that different ranking results will occur when various types of models are compared with different evaluation criteria (Nikooienejad et al., 2020). However, in this study, the EN regression model almost outperformed all the other models in terms of R2 and error indicators; hence, the EN model can be selected as the most appropriate algorithm for predicting breast weight.

sec.title: CONCLUSIONS
sec.p: This study demonstrates that in vivo measurements of broiler pectoral muscle by B-mode ultrasound imagers, with traditionally measured in vivo traits, including LW, BW, and the length of the fossil bone, can be imported into the EN algorithm to establish a reliable model, providing an accurate prediction of the weight of breast muscle in broilers. This method can result in significant savings in money and time cost for slaughter and has a beneficial effect on the enhancement of the breeding process. However, the regular training of surveyors is required to ensure the reliability of the data. In addition, more machine learning and deep learning algorithms deserve further evaluation.

sec.title: ACKNOWLEDGMENTS
sec.p: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 32230101), National Key R&D Plan (No.2022YFF1000203), China Agriculture Research System of MOF and MARA (CARS-41), and the Agricultural Science and Technology Innovation Program (ASTIP-IAS04 and CAASZDRW202005).

sec.title: DISCLOSURES
sec.p: All authors disclosed no relevant relationships.

fn.p: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.psj.2023.102911.
